one_line_takeaway: IPI's real bottleneck is getting malicious text retrieved at all -- a cheap ($0.21/query) black-box cross-entropy-method optimizer that learns a 5-10 token prefix drives near-100% Recall@5 across 11 BEIR corpora and 8 embedding models, and in a realistic Enron-email multi-agent pipeline this single poisoned email pushes GPT-4o's SSH-key-exfiltration success rate to 80%, while query paraphrasing, perplexity filtering, and token masking all fail against an adaptive attacker.
key_finding: On the 11 BEIR datasets, the Vanilla baseline (no trigger) is never retrieved, while CEM reaches near-100% Recall@5 with as few as 5-10 tokens on most corpora (e.g. NFCorpus, Natural Questions, SciFact, HotpotQA, DBPedia, SciDocs, FEVER) and exceeds 80-90% on harder corpora (MS MARCO, ArguAna) with ~15 tokens, versus Query+ which stalls around 20% Recall@5 on ArguAna even at longer lengths. Attack cost is $0.21 per query on OpenAI/Voyage embeddings (up to $0.76 on Qwen text-embedding-v4) and 1.6-7.6 minutes on a single H100 for open-source models. The attack generalizes across 8 embedding models on FiQA (Recall@5 90-100% for all except one outlier at 90%), transfers moderately within model families but poorly across some families (e.g. a Qwen3-0.6B-optimized trigger yields only 10% recall on OpenAI's model), and remains largely position-agnostic and robust to token dispersion (Recall@5 stays 68-91% on most models when trigger tokens are scattered randomly, except OpenAI's embedding which collapses to 6%). In the RAG setting, a single poisoned document drives ASR near 1 for most of the 11 LLMs across 11 datasets, with instruction-tuned and larger models generally more (not less) vulnerable. In the agentic email case study, a single poisoned email coerced GPT-4o into a code-execution/SSH-key-exfiltration attack with an ASR of 72% in the single-agent setting and 80% in the multi-agent (Magentic-One) setting -- roughly 40x higher than the single-agent ASR of ~2%, versus an 'Ideal' (already-in-context) baseline that only reaches 58% ASR on GPT-4o in the multi-agent setting, showing that prior 'already in context' IPI evaluations can significantly under- or over-estimate real risk. All three tested defenses (query paraphrasing, perplexity filtering, token masking) provide only small, non-durable gains and are defeated by simple adaptive variants of the attack (e.g. repeating the malicious text lowers its perplexity below that of clean documents; jointly optimizing over paraphrases restores full attack performance).
core_contribution: The paper identifies retrieval itself (not payload crafting) as the real bottleneck for indirect prompt injection (IPI) attacks and shows that under natural user queries and realistic external corpora, unoptimized malicious text is essentially never retrieved. To close this gap, the authors decompose an injected item into a 'trigger fragment' (a short token prefix whose sole job is to guarantee retrieval for a given query) and an 'attack fragment' (the arbitrary malicious payload), and propose a black-box optimizer -- a factorized, tailored variant of the Cross-Entropy Method (CEM) -- that constructs the trigger fragment using only API access to the embedding model (no gradients, no knowledge of the corpus). They prove an (epsilon,delta)-utility guarantee for the algorithm under a linear/factorized scoring-function assumption (proof deferred to an appendix), and then run the first end-to-end IPI evaluation spanning retrieval, RAG generation, and single-/multi-agent tool-use pipelines, plus an evaluation of three retrieval-stage defenses.
threat_model: Black-box adversary with no access to corpus contents (beyond injecting one item), no access to retriever or LLM parameters, and only standard API query access to the embedding model E. The adversary is restricted to inserting a single malicious item (x || D_adv) into the external corpus, simulating a stealth, unverified-write scenario such as an email inbox or web-writable knowledge base. Attack objective: given an arbitrary natural user query q and an attacker-chosen attack fragment D_adv (encoding e.g. misinformation, phishing, or a shell command), construct a short prefix x such that x||D_adv ranks in the retriever's top-K for q, so that D_adv enters the LLM's context and is executed. White-box gradient attacks (e.g. HotFlip) and prior 'idealized' IPI setups that assume the malicious text is already in context (e.g. 'read my latest email') are explicitly excluded as unrealistic/out of scope.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) evaluation is strictly limited to embedding-based retrieval; hybrid retrieval pipelines and reranking mechanisms, which could plausibly mitigate the attack, are not evaluated. (2) Transferability across embedding models with different architectures is not guaranteed and is left as future work (Figure 5 shows some cross-family transfers as low as 10% recall). (3) The core CEM optimization is explicitly noted as an adaptation of a pre-existing classic algorithm (cross-entropy method), not a novel optimizer -- the paper's novelty is the IPI-specific formulation/application and the end-to-end evaluation. (4) The theoretical (epsilon,delta)-utility guarantee (Theorem 1) requires the scoring function to have a linear/factorized structure across token positions, an assumption the authors justify only empirically (via pooling behavior of modern embedding models) rather than proving holds generally. (5) Defenses evaluated are restricted to retrieval-stage, non-fine-tuning defenses; parameter-modifying defenses (SecAlign, DataSentinel, StruQ) are explicitly out of scope because the evaluation targets closed-source proprietary LLMs. (6) The attack fragments/payloads themselves are drawn from prior work, not newly designed by this paper.

quantitative_results: Vanilla (no trigger) baseline: 0% retrieval on all 11 BEIR datasets
CEM trigger (5-10 tokens): near-100% Recall@5 on NFCorpus, Natural Questions, SciFact, HotpotQA, DBPedia, SciDocs, FEVER
CEM trigger (~15 tokens): >80%, sometimes >90% Recall@5 on MS MARCO and ArguAna
Query+ baseline on ArguAna: only ~20% Recall@5 even at longer lengths
Attack cost: as little as $0.21 per target query on OpenAI text-embedding-3-small / Voyage voyage-3.5-lite embeddings; up to $0.76 on Alibaba text-embedding-v4
Open-source runtime on single H100: 1.6 min (Contriever), 2.3 min (GTE), 7.6 min (Qwen3-0.6B)
FiQA cross-embedding-model Recall@5: 90-100% across GTE, Contriever, Qwen3-0.6B/4B/8B, Voyage, OpenAI, Qwen-v4
Token dispersion robustness (FiQA, Recall@5): GTE ~90%, Q3-4B ~91%, Q3-8B/Voyage/Qwen-v4 >80%, OpenAI collapses to 6%
Transferability: OpenAI-optimized trigger generalizes to 7/8 target models above 60% (avg 74% recall); Qwen3-0.6B-optimized trigger yields only 10% recall on OpenAI's model
RAG targeted-answer ASR: near 1 for most of 11 LLMs (Qwen3 0.6B-32B, LLaMA-3 3B/8B base+Ins, Vicuna 7B/13B) across nearly all 11 datasets; MS-MARCO shows lower ASR, consistent with weaker Recall@5
Knowledge-poisoning comparison on NQ with LLaMA-2-7B: prior method (query-prepend) ASR 0.58; this paper's method matches ASR 0.58 with a 2-token trigger, and 0.50 with a 1-token trigger
Single-agent code execution (SSH key exfiltration), GPT-4o: Ideal baseline ASR 0.02, Query+ ASR 0.02, Ours (CEM) ASR 0.04, Ours (Fusion) ASR 0.02 (fairly low in single-agent)

Multi-agent code execution (SSH key exfiltration), GPT-4o: Ideal baseline ASR 0.58, Ours (CEM) ASR 0.72, Ours (Fusion) ASR 0.80 -- described by the authors as ~40x higher than the single-agent GPT-4o ASR (2-4%) and equivalent to 8/10 runs succeeding
Multi-agent phishing-worm propagation, GPT-4o: Ideal ASR 0.01, Query+ ASR 0.08, Ours (CEM) ASR 0.00, Ours (Fusion) ASR 0.18
Multi-agent tool misuse (mass phishing email), GPT-4o: Ideal ASR 1.00, Query+ ASR 0.99, Ours (CEM) ASR 0.92, Ours (Fusion) ASR 0.98
GPT-4o-mini multi-agent code execution ASR: Ideal 0.54, Query+ 0.56, Ours (CEM) 0.42, Ours (Fusion) 0.36
Perplexity filtering: repeating the malicious text drives its perplexity below that of clean documents while preserving attack effectiveness, defeating the filter
Query paraphrasing: single-paraphrase defense causes <10% Recall@5 drop; jointly optimizing over multiple paraphrases fully restores (and sometimes exceeds) baseline attack performance
baselines_compared: Query+ (prepend the user query itself, from Liu et al. 2024 / used in Zhong et al. and Zou et al. RAG-poisoning works)
Ideal baseline (attack fragment assumed already in context, per AgentDojo-style evaluation)
White-box gradient attacks (HotFlip, [117]) -- excluded from experiments as out of the paper's black-box threat model, discussed only qualitatively
